prompt: A woman in her 40s gets a routine screening mammogram. Her result comes back positive. What is the probability (in %) that she actually has breast cancer?
source: Gigerenzer, Reckoning with Risk (2002), ch. 4: prevalence ≈1%, sensitivity ≈80%, false-positive rate ≈9.6% → PPV ≈7.8%. Consistent with modern Breast Cancer Surveillance Consortium data (~5–10% PPV per recalled screen for women in this age group).
revealHeadline: A positive test isn't the same as a diagnosis.
revealExplanation: Across 1,000 women in this age group: ~10 have breast cancer (low base rate). The test correctly catches ~8 of them. But it also falsely flags ~95 of the 990 who DON'T have cancer. So ~103 women get a positive result, and only ~8 of those actually have cancer: roughly 8%. The intuition trap is anchoring on ‘the test is 80% accurate’ and forgetting how rare the disease is to begin with.
